01_initial_request.input_data: protein subunit sequences and a dataset of 57 protein complexes
01_initial_request.user_intent: Predict the structures of large protein complexes with six to twenty chains using a reinforcement learning framework
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Average RMSD of 10.09 Å and TM-SCORE of 0.81

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Large protein complex structures are needed to understand molecular mechanisms, but experimental determination is difficult for complexes with multiple chains. [Gap] A benchmark dataset of large protein complexes (6-20 chains) is required to evaluate the new method. [Decision] To achieve reliable benchmarking, chose to use PDB Database to perform dataset selection on protein complexes, expecting to obtain 57 complexes with six to twenty chains.

parameters.selection_criteria: complexes with 6 to 15 chains (extended to 20 in abstract)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Dataset of 57 protein complexes constructed for evaluation.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The success of assembly depends on the quality and diversity of pairwise models. [Gap] Standard pairwise prediction may miss critical interfaces or lack diversity. [Decision] To achieve diverse pairwise candidates, chose to use AlphaFold-Multimer to perform pairwise docking with forced sampling on subunit pairs, expecting to obtain 75 decoys per pair.

parameters.input_strategy: duplicated copies of one chain (e.g., A1B3)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Pool of pairwise decoys generated for each pair of chains with 88.29% average interacting pairwise coverage.

references.citation: Evans, R. et al. Protein complex prediction with AlphaFold-Multimer. bioRxiv (2021). [21]
references.relevance: Provides the base tool for pairwise structure prediction.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Pairwise decoys need to be assembled into a full complex structure without clashes. [Gap] Combinatorial explosion makes exhaustive search impossible for large complexes. [Decision] To achieve efficient assembly, chose to use AFM-RL (Reinforcement Learning) to perform decoy assembly on pairwise pools, expecting to obtain 1500 to 12,000 assembled models per target.

parameters.scoring_terms: molecular mechanics potential, solvent accessible surface area, radius of gyration, atom clash counts

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Assembled complex models generated (1500 to 12,000 per target) with rewards propagated to Q-table.

references.citation: Kocsis, L. & Szepesvári, C. Bandit Based Monte-Carlo Planning. (2006). [24]
references.relevance: Supports the UCB policy used for assembly state selection.

references.citation: Tokic, M. Adaptive -greedy exploration in reinforcement learning... (2010). [25]
references.relevance: Supports the e-greedy policy used for decoy selection.

references.citation: Aderinwale, T. et al. RL-MLZerD: Multimeric protein docking using reinforcement learning. (2022). [10]
references.relevance: Previous work establishing the RL framework foundation.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The generated models contain redundancy and need to be grouped by structural similarity. [Gap] Raw model output is too large to evaluate individually. [Decision] To achieve representative model selection, chose to use LB3DClust to perform clustering on assembled models, expecting to obtain clustered groups of structures.

references.citation: Terashi, G. et al. LB3D: A Protein Three-Dimensional Substructure Search Program... (2012). [27]
references.relevance: Provides the clustering algorithm used to reduce model redundancy.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Clustered models need to be ranked to identify the best prediction. [Gap] Geometric quality and energy scores need to be combined for final selection. [Decision] To achieve optimal model identification, chose to use VoroMQA to perform quality assessment and ranking on clustered models, expecting to obtain ranked representative models.

parameters.ranking_method: sum of score ranks by the scoring function and the VoroMQA score
parameters.selection: best scoring model selected as the representative for each cluster

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Ranked representative models selected for each cluster.

references.citation: Olechnovič, K. & Venclovas, Č. VoroMQA: Assessment of protein structure quality... (2017). [28]
references.relevance: Provides the quality assessment score used for final ranking.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The predicted structures need to be validated against experimental native structures. [Gap] Quantitative metrics are required to measure prediction accuracy. [Decision] To achieve performance validation, chose to use MMAlign to perform RMSD and TM-score calculation on predicted complexes, expecting to obtain accuracy metrics.

parameters.metrics: Full complex RMSD, Partial RMSD, TM-score
parameters.comparison: against native structure and MoLPC/AFM methods

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Average RMSD of 10.09 Å and TM-SCORE of 0.81 achieved across 57 targets.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Comparison of predicted structures against experimental native structures using RMSD and TM-score, and benchmarking against MoLPC and AlphaFold-Multimer.

average_rmsd.interpretation: Average root mean square deviation of the best-assembled model across all targets, lower than MoLPC's 18.63 Å.

average_tm_score.interpretation: Average TM-SCORE of the best-assembled model across all targets, higher than MoLPC's 0.69.

partial_rmsd.interpretation: Average partial RMSD calculated by MMAlign, compared to 5.71 Å of MoLPC.

residue_coverage_ratio.interpretation: Average ratio of sequence included in partial RMSD calculation, compared to 66% of MoLPC.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was achieved. AFM-RL successfully predicted structures of large protein complexes (6-20 chains) with an average RMSD of 10.09 Å and TM-SCORE of 0.81, outperforming existing methods like MoLPC.
